name: Vanuatu
tagline: Eighty islands of ash, coral and kastom
summary: Vanuatu is a Melanesian archipelago of some eighty volcanic and coral islands in the southwestern Pacific, settled around three thousand years ago by Austronesian seafarers of the Lapita culture whose oldest known Pacific cemetery lies at Teouma on Efate. Its oral history reveres the paramount chief Roi Mata, whose seventeenth-century domain is now a UNESCO World Heritage site. After European contact from 1606, the islands endured the sandalwood trade, the 'blackbirding' labour traffic and catastrophic depopulation by disease, then seven decades under a famously duplicated Anglo-French Condominium known as the New Hebrides. Vanuatu became an independent republic on 30 July 1980 and today, vulnerable to cyclones and rising seas, leads the world's climate diplomacy.

founding.label: Independence as the Republic of Vanuatu
founding.detail: The New Hebrides, jointly governed by Britain and France, became the independent Republic of Vanuatu, with the Anglican priest Walter Lini of the Vanua'aku Pati as its first prime minister.

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: ~80 islands on the Pacific 'Ring of Fire' and cyclone belt

eras.title: Lapita Seafarers and Chief Roi Mata
eras.standfirst: Austronesian voyagers of the Lapita culture peopled the archipelago, and from their descendants rose the paramount chief Roi Mata, remembered in oral tradition and honoured by a World Heritage site.
body: Vanuatu was among the last large island groups of the western Pacific to be reached by people. By about 1300–1000 BCE seafarers of the Lapita culture, part of the great Austronesian expansion out of island Southeast Asia, had crossed the open ocean and settled the northern islands, bringing distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, domesticated plants and animals. At Teouma on south Efate archaeologists uncovered a cemetery dated to roughly 3,000 years ago — the oldest known burial ground in the Pacific — its graves a direct window onto the first colonists who hunted now-extinct tortoises, land crocodiles and flightless birds.

Over the following millennia successive migrations layered the archipelago with extraordinary diversity; Vanuatu became one of the most linguistically dense places on Earth, with more than a hundred indigenous languages. Polynesian voyagers later settled the southern outliers of Aniwa and Futuna. Society was organised around chiefs and the grade-taking systems by which men rose in rank, and around the deep web of custom — kastom — that still governs land, kinship and ceremony across the islands.

Out of this world came Roi Mata, a paramount chief of central Vanuatu remembered in a rich oral tradition as a unifier who imposed peace among warring clans and a system of moieties to end bloodshed. According to tradition he lived around the early seventeenth century, ruling lands across Efate, Lelepa and the islet of Artok. He was buried with retainers on Artok, and respect for him survives in four centuries of prohibitions on his key sites — a living link between memory and place.

events.title: Lapita seafarers settle the archipelago
events.summary: Austronesian voyagers of the Lapita culture reached northern Vanuatu, carrying their distinctive pottery, crops and animals on one of history's great oceanic migrations.

events.title: The Teouma cemetery on Efate
events.summary: The burial ground at Teouma on Efate, dated to about 3,000 years ago, is the oldest known cemetery in the Pacific and preserves the remains of the first settlers.

events.title: Reign of the paramount chief Roi Mata
events.summary: Oral tradition records Roi Mata as a great chief of central Vanuatu who ended inter-clan warfare and whose burial on Artok is honoured by lasting prohibitions on his sites.

figures.name: Roi Mata
figures.blurb: Melanesian paramount chief remembered in oral tradition for uniting warring clans and ending bloodshed; his residence, death site and mass burial form the UNESCO World Heritage property named for him.

pullquote.text: Four centuries on, the people still keep his places forbidden — oral memory made law upon the land.
pullquote.attribution: On the legacy of Chief Roi Mata

eras.title: European Contact and Naming
eras.standfirst: A Spanish expedition mistook Espiritu Santo for the unknown southern continent in 1606; Bougainville and then Cook, who named the islands the New Hebrides, followed in the eighteenth century.
body: The first Europeans to sight the archipelago arrived in May 1606 with the Portuguese-born navigator Pedro Fernández de Quirós, commanding a Spanish expedition in search of the fabled Terra Australis Incognita. Convinced he had found the great southern continent, Quirós landed on the largest island, named it La Austrialia del Espíritu Santo — the Southern Land of the Holy Spirit — and claimed it and the whole Pacific to the Pole for Spain. He tried to found a settlement, 'New Jerusalem', but the colony collapsed within weeks amid the islanders' hostility and quarrels among his crew, and the expedition withdrew. The island still bears the name Espiritu Santo.

For more than a century and a half the islands lay beyond European reach. In 1768 the French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville passed through, charting parts of the group and disproving Quirós's continental claim by sailing past the supposed coastline. The encounters were fleeting; no European power moved to occupy the islands, whose peoples continued their own histories largely undisturbed by the outside world.

In July 1774 the British captain James Cook, on his second Pacific voyage in the Resolution, surveyed the archipelago over several weeks, with his longest contacts on Malakula and Tanna. Cook charted the islands and gave them the collective name New Hebrides, after the Hebrides off Scotland — a name they would carry for two hundred years until independence. His maps brought the group firmly onto European charts and opened the way to the traders and beachcombers who soon followed.

events.title: Quirós reaches Espiritu Santo
events.summary: Pedro Fernández de Quirós, leading a Spanish expedition, landed on the largest island, named it La Austrialia del Espíritu Santo and claimed it for Spain before his short-lived colony failed.

events.title: Bougainville sails through the group
events.summary: The French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville charted parts of the archipelago and showed that Quirós's 'southern continent' was a group of islands.

events.title: Cook names the New Hebrides
events.summary: On his second voyage James Cook surveyed and charted the islands, naming them the New Hebrides — a name they kept until independence in 1980.

figures.name: Pedro Fernández de Quirós
figures.blurb: Led the 1606 expedition that first brought Europeans to the islands, naming Espiritu Santo in the mistaken belief he had found the great southern continent.

eras.title: Sandalwood, Blackbirding and Missions
eras.standfirst: Sandalwood traders, the 'blackbirding' labour traffic and introduced disease devastated island populations, while Christian missionaries — some martyred — transformed island life.
body: From the 1820s the islands were drawn violently into the world economy. Traders stripped the archipelago of its sandalwood for the China market, and as the stands were exhausted attention turned to the islanders themselves. From the 1860s 'blackbirding' recruiters carried tens of thousands of men — and some women — to labour on the sugar plantations of Queensland and Fiji and the mines of New Caledonia. Some went willingly under indenture; many were deceived or seized outright. At the height of the trade more than half the adult men of some islands were away, and between 1863 and 1904 over forty thousand islanders were taken to Queensland alone.

The human cost compounded a demographic catastrophe. Measles, dysentery, influenza and other diseases against which the islanders had no immunity, introduced by traders and labour ships, swept through communities with appalling lethality. Across the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries the population of the archipelago is thought to have collapsed to a fraction of its earlier size, hollowing out whole districts and reshaping the islands' societies.

Into this upheaval came Christian missionaries, chiefly Presbyterian and Anglican, from 1839. Their early reception was often fatal: John Williams of the London Missionary Society was killed at Erromango in 1839, and other missionaries followed him to violent deaths. Yet over the decades mission churches, schools and printing presses spread across the islands, Christianity took deep root, and missionaries became among the loudest critics of the abuses of the labour trade — a paradox of destruction and conversion that defined the colonial century.

events.title: Missionaries arrive; Williams killed at Erromango
events.summary: Christian missions began in the islands; the LMS missionary John Williams was killed at Erromango, the first of several martyrdoms as Christianity slowly took hold.

events.title: The blackbirding labour trade
events.summary: Recruiters carried over forty thousand islanders to Queensland and others to Fiji and New Caledonia for plantation labour, by indenture, deception and force.

events.title: Depopulation by introduced disease
events.summary: Measles, dysentery, influenza and other introduced diseases, together with the labour trade, devastated island populations across the colonial period.

pullquote.text: Traders took first the sandalwood, then the people themselves; the diseases they left behind took the rest.

eras.title: The Anglo-French Condominium
eras.standfirst: Britain and France governed the New Hebrides jointly under a uniquely duplicated condominium, while wartime American bases helped spawn the John Frum cargo movement on Tanna.
body: Rivalry between British and French settlers led the two powers, by a convention of 1906, to govern the New Hebrides together as an Anglo-French Condominium. The arrangement was extraordinary: almost everything was duplicated. There were British and French resident commissioners, two police forces, two currencies, two systems of law and courts, separate British and French schools and hospitals, even separate prisons — alongside a joint administration for matters held in common. The ni-Vanuatu themselves, governed but not citizens of either power, fell between the cracks of this 'Pandemonium', as it was sometimes mockingly called, with their land alienated to planters and little say in their own affairs.

The Second World War shattered the islands' isolation. After 1942 the United States built vast bases on Efate and Espiritu Santo, through which hundreds of thousands of servicemen and a flood of manufactured goods passed. The sudden abundance, and the spectacle of Black American troops treated as equals, left a deep impression. On Tanna it fused with older prophetic movements into the John Frum cargo cult, whose followers awaited the return of a messianic figure — 'John from America' — who would bring wealth and restore custom against missionaries and colonial rule.

After the war pressure for change grew. Land grievances, the spread of education, and a generation of ni-Vanuatu leaders fed a rising nationalism that the cumbersome dual administration was ill-equipped to contain. By the 1970s movements for self-government and independence were taking organised political form, setting Britain and France — who often disagreed sharply over the islands' future — on the road to decolonisation.

events.title: Anglo-French Condominium established
events.summary: Britain and France agreed to govern the New Hebrides jointly, creating a famously duplicated administration with two of nearly everything.

events.title: U.S. wartime bases on Efate and Santo
events.summary: American forces built major bases in the islands during the Pacific War, flooding them with goods and personnel and transforming local society.

events.title: Rise of the John Frum movement
events.summary: On Tanna the John Frum cargo cult emerged, awaiting a messianic 'John from America' who would bring cargo and restore custom against missionary and colonial control.

pullquote.text: Two flags, two currencies, two police forces, two laws — and a people who belonged to neither.

eras.title: Independence and the Coconut War
eras.standfirst: The Vanua'aku Pati under Walter Lini led the New Hebrides to independence as Vanuatu in 1980, even as a secessionist revolt on Espiritu Santo — the 'Coconut War' — challenged the new state.
body: Organised nationalism crystallised around the New Hebrides National Party, founded in 1971 and led by the Anglican priest Walter Lini. Renamed the Vanua'aku Pati ('Our Land Party'), it demanded immediate independence and the return of land alienated to European planters, drawing strong support from the English-speaking, Protestant population. France and many French settlers, fearing a Lini-led state, backed rival francophone parties, and the path to independence became entangled with the old condominium's Anglo-French divisions.

Self-government came in 1978 and elections in 1979 gave the Vanua'aku Pati a clear majority. As independence approached, opposition crystallised on Espiritu Santo, where Jimmy Stevens's Nagriamel movement — encouraged by French settlers and foreign business interests — proclaimed the breakaway 'State of Vemerana'. The rebels, armed largely with bows, spears and stones, blockaded the airport and cut bridges in the episode the press dubbed the 'Coconut War'.

The Republic of Vanuatu nonetheless came into being on 30 July 1980, with Walter Lini as its first prime minister. To end the secession, his government called in troops from Papua New Guinea, who swiftly suppressed the revolt in August; Jimmy Stevens was tried and imprisoned. Independent Vanuatu adopted a foreign policy of non-alignment and 'Melanesian socialism', joining the Non-Aligned Movement and establishing ties across Cold War lines before opening relations with the United States in 1986.

events.title: Vanua'aku Pati founded under Walter Lini
events.summary: The party that became the Vanua'aku Pati, led by the Anglican priest Walter Lini, was formed to demand independence and the return of alienated land.

events.title: The Coconut War on Espiritu Santo
events.summary: Jimmy Stevens's Nagriamel movement declared the breakaway 'State of Vemerana' on Espiritu Santo; the lightly armed revolt was suppressed soon after independence.

events.title: Republic of Vanuatu proclaimed
events.summary: The New Hebrides became the independent Republic of Vanuatu, with Walter Lini as first prime minister; Papua New Guinean troops then crushed the Santo secession.

figures.name: Walter Lini
figures.blurb: Anglican priest who founded the Vanua'aku Pati, led the independence movement and governed independent Vanuatu from 1980, championing non-alignment and 'Melanesian socialism'.

pullquote.text: On 30 July 1980 the New Hebrides — two of everything for seventy years — became one nation: Vanuatu.

eras.title: Kastom, Cyclones and Climate Diplomacy
eras.standfirst: Independent Vanuatu balances custom and democracy, weathers ever-fiercer cyclones and rising seas, and has made itself the global standard-bearer for climate justice.
body: Since independence Vanuatu has remained a parliamentary republic in which custom — kastom — retains real authority, with a National Council of Chiefs (the Malvatumauri) advising on tradition and land. Politics has been lively and often fragmented, with frequent changes of government, yet the state has endured and democratic transfers of power have been peaceful. In 2008 UNESCO inscribed Chief Roi Mata's Domain — the chief's residence, death site and burial on Efate, Lelepa and Artok — as Vanuatu's first World Heritage property, recognising the living bond between oral tradition and place.

Geography makes Vanuatu acutely exposed. Straddling the Pacific 'Ring of Fire' and the cyclone belt, it is repeatedly ranked among the world's most disaster-prone countries. Tropical Cyclone Pam in March 2015 was the most destructive storm in its history, damaging the great majority of buildings and a large share of national output; Cyclone Harold followed in April 2020, striking amid the COVID-19 pandemic. Rising seas now threaten low-lying coastal villages, forcing some communities to consider relocation inland.

Out of this vulnerability Vanuatu has built outsized influence. A campaign begun by Pacific law students in 2019 and championed by the Vanuatu government persuaded the UN General Assembly in March 2023 to ask the International Court of Justice for an advisory opinion on states' legal obligations on climate change. The Court delivered its landmark opinion on 23 July 2025, finding that states have binding duties to protect the climate and that failure to act can be an internationally wrongful act — a diplomatic triumph that Vanuatu now seeks to turn into UN political action.

events.title: Chief Roi Mata's Domain joins World Heritage List
events.summary: UNESCO inscribed the residence, death site and burial of Chief Roi Mata on Efate, Lelepa and Artok — Vanuatu's first World Heritage property.

events.title: Cyclone Pam devastates the islands
events.summary: Tropical Cyclone Pam, the most destructive storm in Vanuatu's history, damaged the great majority of buildings and a vast share of national output.

events.title: UN asks the ICJ on climate obligations
events.summary: A Vanuatu-led campaign, begun by Pacific law students, won a UN General Assembly resolution requesting an ICJ advisory opinion on states' climate-change obligations.

events.title: ICJ delivers landmark climate opinion
events.summary: The International Court of Justice found that states have binding legal duties to protect the climate and that failure to act may be an internationally wrongful act.

figures.name: Walter Lini
figures.blurb: First leader of independent Vanuatu, who set the young republic's course of non-alignment, custom and Pacific solidarity that still shapes its climate diplomacy.

pullquote.text: A nation of eighty islands, among the world's most exposed to the warming sea, has become the conscience of global climate law.

figures.name: Roi Mata
figures.blurb: Melanesian paramount chief remembered in oral tradition for uniting warring clans and ending bloodshed; the UNESCO World Heritage property named for him preserves his residence, death site and burial.

figures.name: Pedro Fernández de Quirós
figures.role: Navigator who brought first European contact
figures.blurb: Portuguese-born navigator in Spanish service who reached the islands in 1606 and named Espiritu Santo, mistaking it for the great southern continent.

figures.name: Walter Lini
figures.blurb: Anglican priest who founded the Vanua'aku Pati, led the New Hebrides to independence in 1980 and governed the new republic on a course of non-alignment and Melanesian socialism.
